name: Participant Inclusion Pathway and Yield Threshold
consequences: Defining which parasomnia severity, frequency, and diagnostic certainty gates admission directly controls the proportion of participants who actually capture events during their stay. Stricter pre-screening increases yield but narrows generalizability and extends recruitment cycles; looser criteria broaden phenotypic coverage but risk higher unproductive admissions, exhausting the 20% ceiling and forcing protocol revision by month 12.
options: Require documented seizure-free EEG and prior polysomnographic evidence of at least one confirmed parasomnia event before admission, accepting slower recruitment but higher event yield and reduced ambiguity in adjudication.

Accept clinical diagnosis from sleep specialist referral with collateral history alone, no mandatory prior PSG, lowering pre-admission screening burden and accelerating recruitment but increasing risk of non-parasomnia mimics and unproductive admissions.

Implement a two-tier pathway: standard tier requires prior PSG confirmation (higher yield cohort), while expanded tier accepts specialist diagnosis without prior polysomnography and is capped at 40% of total enrollment, explicitly stratifying analysis by diagnostic certainty.
review: Controls recruitment speed and diagnostic purity vs. participant diversity and phenotypic breadth. Weakness: Options do not address attrition post-admission due to lack of event capture during stay, which may waste facility capacity even if baseline selection is sound.

name: Length-of-Stay Protocol and Extension Logic
consequences: Current protocol permits stays from 2–8 weeks with conditional extension to 10 weeks for zero-event cases. This directly shapes staffing demand, participant burden, and cumulative event sample size. Longer baseline stays increase event probability but amplify dropout risk, accommodation cost per participant, and coordinatorial overhead; shorter stays with aggressive extension triggers may miss late-presenting clusters and fragment participant commitment.
options: Fix baseline stay at 4 weeks for all participants regardless of event capture, with one optional two-week extension only if requested by participant and facility capacity permits, reducing scheduling complexity and per-participant cost but accepting lower absolute yield.

Adopt a dynamic tiering: participants with 2+ events in first 10 days are discharged after 3 weeks; those with zero events are automatically offered 6–8 weeks, with protocol exit if facilities are overbooked, prioritizing data richness over uniform duration and participant choice.

Implement a rolling cohort model with 3-week baseline stays, but offer a 6-month remote actigraphy follow-up with monthly in-person PSG nights for zero-event participants, decoupling intensive facility occupancy from longitudinal phenotyping and reducing acute bed pressure.
review: Controls cost-per-event and facility throughput vs. event-capture completeness and participant convenience. Weakness: The options fail to model staffing scalability and night-technician annotation throughput if stays exceed 6 weeks, which may become a hidden bottleneck.

name: Domestic Environment Fidelity and Safety Trade-off
consequences: The facility must suppress clinical inhibition through residential aesthetics while maintaining episode safety via padded corridors, door alarms, and restricted windows. Increasing safety engineering (e.g., monitored exits, more padding) enhances staff responsiveness but risks signaling clinical surveillance, reducing naturalistic behavior; minimalist safety measures preserve ecological validity but may delay response to genuine injuries and create liability exposure.
options: Prioritize domestic aesthetic over engineered safety: use standard bedroom furniture, minimal visible equipment, no alarms or restricted windows, rely entirely on video monitoring and staff presence, accepting higher injury risk and potential regulatory objection but maximizing behavioral naturalism.

Implement a 'invisible safety' approach using non-obvious interventions: magnetic door closures, infrared perimeter sensors with staff alerts instead of audible alarms, subtle padding disguised as décor baseboard trim, and trained staff trained to position themselves passively rather than hovering, balancing safety and environment.

Divide the 8 suites into 'research' (high-fidelity domestic, minimal engineering) and 'safety-prioritized' (engineered padding, alarms, staffing proximity) tiers, allowing participants to self-select based on comfort tolerance and explicitly stratifying analysis by environment type.
review: Controls naturalistic behavior capture vs. injury liability and regulatory defensibility. Weakness: The options do not quantify or validate whether the proposed safety measures actually reduce injury rates in similar facilities or whether perceived surveillance affects event frequency.

name: Sensor Fusion Strategy and Data Integration Complexity
consequences: Tiered sensing (nightly low-burden headbands and mattress sensors; scheduled enhanced PSG; escalation PSG post-event) reduces participant burden and operational cost while preserving clinical resolution for event characterization. However, integrating disparate streams (dry EEG, standard PSG, contact-free sensors, video, audio) into BIDS-compatible format introduces technical debt, synchronization risk, and annotation overhead; overly ambitious sensor scope may exceed the data engineer's pipeline capacity or delay event-triage algorithm development.
options: Implement full sensor fusion: all six streams (dry EEG, PSG, mattress, infrared, microphone, environmental) collected on enhanced nights, all synchronized in real time, with automated BIDS conversion on ingest, accepting maximum technical complexity but enabling multi-modal event characterization.

Adopt a hierarchical acquisition model: nightly dry EEG and mattress as primary streams, enhanced PSG and video as secondary, with audio and environmental sensors on escalation nights only, reducing synchronization complexity and prioritizing PSG-video alignment for algorithm training.

Maintain minimal nightly ensemble (dry EEG and mattress only) and reserve full PSG exclusively to pre-scheduled nights (first, mid, final) plus escalation, leaving audio and environmental sensors for future expansion, simplifying infrastructure and deferring sensor fusion challenges.
review: Controls data richness and event-characterization depth vs. technical scalability and time-to-first-publications. Weakness: The options do not address whether dry-electrode EEG and contact-free mattress sensors possess sufficient fidelity to detect parasomnia substrates (e.g., slow-wave density, muscle atonia loss) that inform Aim 2 phenotyping.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development and Validation Scope
consequences: Aim 3 requires a semi-automated tool that ranks probable episodes and reduces manual review burden. Scope ranges from a simple threshold-based flagging system (high specificity, low sensitivity) to a machine-learning classifier trained on dual human scores (higher sensitivity, operational complexity). Broader scope increases publications and grant-leverage potential but delays operational deployment and risks overfitting to small event samples if capture falls short of projections.
options: Deploy a rule-based triage tool by month 12 (e.g., movement+EMG desynchrony+arousal thresholds), validated against dual human scoring but without retraining, accepting lower sensitivity and a 'human-in-the-loop' workflow but achieving rapid operational integration.

Develop an iterative machine-learning classifier trained on captured events from the first 30–40 participants, re-validated against independent scorer agreement, with documented performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) and explicit model limitations, accepting 18-month development timeline and publication burden.

Propose event-triage as a separate Phase-Two grant aim: maintain dual human scoring throughout the 3-year pilot, generate methodological manuscripts on inter-rater reliability and manual annotation burden, and position algorithm development as a future DFG continuation, deferring technical risk.
review: Controls algorithm maturity and operational efficiency gains vs. methodological rigor and honest reporting of validation limitations. Weakness: The options do not specify minimum event sample size required for machine-learning classifier generalization or address the risk that rare event subtypes may be underrepresented in training data.

name: Staffing Coverage Model and Rotational Resilience
consequences: Current plan: 3 technicians rotating night shifts for 8 active suites. This provides 2.7 technicians per suite averaged, permitting real-time annotation and event response. Increasing headcount improves safety margins and annotation timeliness but inflates personnel costs (€2M over three years already); reducing coverage or cross-training non-sleep specialists creates annotation quality risk and liability if an event is missed or mishandled.
options: Hire 4 technicians (one float) to maintain 3-person coverage on high-occupancy nights and build resilience against illness or turnover, accepting 15–20% staffing cost increase but reducing single-point-of-failure risk and enabling on-call escalation.

Retain 3 technicians but implement a part-time annotation squad of 2–3 final-year sleep medicine students or postgraduates hired at €12–15/hour for daytime secondary scoring, preserving headcount while outsourcing rater burden and offering training pipeline, but introducing oversight complexity.

Maintain 3 technicians and mandate cross-training of the study coordinator and clinical psychologist in night-shift backup, accepting their primary-role deprioritization on high-demand nights but eliminating external hiring and reducing payroll while creating internal redundancy.
review: Controls overnight safety and annotation response speed vs. payroll flexibility and cost containment. Weakness: The options do not address quality control mechanisms to ensure that part-time annotators or cross-trained staff produce inter-rater reliability meeting project thresholds.

name: Data Sharing and Public Repository Participation
consequences: Current plan limits public data to de-identified physiological and sensor streams under data use agreements; video is excluded due to privacy. This satisfies GDPR and ethical guardrails but restricts verification of event annotation by external researchers and may limit citation/impact of deposited datasets. Expanding open sharing increases methodological transparency and reproducibility but demands additional privacy-impact assessment and potential regulatory friction; maintaining restricted access avoids controversy but risks perception of selective data availability.
options: Deposit only dual-human-scored event annotations (metadata: timestamp, event type, morphology codes) plus corresponding non-video sensor excerpts in a public repository, allowing external validation of triage algorithm performance without raw video exposure and balancing transparency with privacy.

Establish a controlled-access repository through a partner institution (e.g., German Data Center for Sleep Research), requiring Data Use Agreements for all access and hosting pseudo-anonymized video alongside physiological data, enabling full external audit but introducing governance complexity and access latency.

Restrict public deposit to processed derivatives (averaged spectral features, movement indices, arousal density per night) and event-annotation metadata, deferring raw sensor data to a university-hosted archive available to collaborators on request, minimizing privacy risk while maintaining academic reproducibility.
review: Controls transparency and external validation opportunity vs. privacy assurance and regulatory simplicity. Weakness: The options do not specify whether external researchers can access dual-human-scoring ground truth for algorithm benchmarking, which may undermine Aim 3's reproducibility claim.

name: Residential Authenticity Versus Clinical Instrumentalization
consequences: The facility's domestic appearance directly influences whether participants exhibit naturalistic parasomnia expression or suppress behaviors due to perceived clinical surveillance. Excessive equipment visibility, institutional aesthetics, or rigid routines may artificially lower event frequency and distort phenotyping data. Conversely, minimizing instrumentation visibility increases operational risk and complicates real-time technician response to events.
options: Design two interior zones: private sleeping quarters stripped of visible equipment with cameras and sensors concealed behind aesthetic finishes, and a separate technician monitoring station with full display arrays, requiring participants to accept that hidden monitoring exists but is perceptually absent from the bedroom itself.

Integrate all sensing infrastructure openly as part of the residential aesthetic—mounted cameras as design features, EEG headbands customized to appear as sleepwear accessories, mattress sensors presented as comfort technology—and measure whether transparency about instrumentation affects event yield or phenotypic distribution compared to the initial cohort.

Adopt a 'baseline-then-instrument' protocol where participants spend the first 3 nights in a minimally equipped residential suite with only actigraphy and ambient audio, then transition to the full sensor suite for nights 4 onward, allowing within-subject comparison of behavior naturalism and event capture rates.
review: Controls trade-off between suppression of naturalistic behavior under surveillance vs. operational safety and data quality assurance. Weakness: The options do not address how technician response times scale with concealed equipment or whether aesthetic integration strategies inadvertently introduce noise or artifact in sensor fidelity.

name: Participant Length-of-Stay Calibration and Episode Yield Economics
consequences: The current protocol allows stays of 2–8 weeks with extension up to 10 weeks if zero events are captured; this flexibility increases the probability of capturing infrequent events but inflates per-participant direct costs (meals, compensation, technician time) and may saturate facility bed-days before reaching the 50–70 enrollment target. Earlier discharge thresholds reduce cost per participant but risk high 'unproductive admission' rates, triggering recruitment-criteria reviews that delay data collection.
options: Adopt a fixed-duration protocol: all participants admit for exactly 3 weeks unless they experience a severe safety incident; use the initial cohort (n=15–20) to establish empirical baseline event-capture probabilities, then calculate optimal stay length to balance cost, enrollment velocity, and Aim 2 statistical power before scaling to years 2–3.

Implement risk-stratified stays: categorize pre-admission participants by reported monthly episode frequency, comorbidity complexity, and prior sleep study yield; assign 'high-yield' participants 2-week stays and 'low-yield' or uncertain participants 4-week stays with flexible extension; use this data to iteratively refine predictive models of who benefits from longer observation.

Institute a dynamic discharge rule: discharge any participant after 10 consecutive nights without a captured event, regardless of total elapsed days; apply this uniformly across all admissions to minimize cost variance and create a clear operational boundary, accepting that some low-frequency episoders may not be captured but reducing false-extension costs.
review: Controls balance between maximizing per-participant event yield and constraining total operational costs within the 3-year budget. Weakness: The options do not quantify the sensitivity of enrollment numbers or publication readiness to different stay-length distributions, nor do they account for seasonal or circadian variation in parasomnia expression that may favor longer stays in specific seasons.

name: Algorithmic Triage Design Philosophy and Validation Rigor
consequences: Aim 3 requires a semi-automated event-triage tool that ranks probable parasomnia episodes to reduce manual review burden; the design philosophy—whether the algorithm emphasizes high sensitivity (capture all probable events, some false positives) or high specificity (minimize false positives, risk missing rare morphologies)—directly affects scoring workload and confidence in negative results. A sensitivity-first approach may overwhelm raters with false alarms; a specificity-first approach may systematically miss clinically important variant expressions.
options: Develop a two-stage triage pipeline: stage one uses high-sensitivity detection (e.g., movement + arousal + EEG features) to generate a broad candidate list; stage two applies a specificity filter trained on dual-rated consensus annotations from the first 30 participants to rank and cluster candidates, with all high-ranked and borderline candidates sent to raters for adjudication, allowing stage two to evolve as the phenotype library grows.

Build a 'confidence-stratified' approach where the algorithm outputs a continuous confidence score rather than binary positive/negative labels; raters prioritize high-confidence candidates and can defer low-confidence ambiguous segments to a secondary pass if time permits, explicitly accepting incomplete annotation of the lowest-confidence events to preserve rater capacity for high-yield nights.

Establish a hybrid validation framework where triage outputs are compared not only to dual human consensus but also to a subset of events independently reviewed by the clinical psychologist and PI to detect systematic biases (e.g., underdetection of confusional arousals versus sleep terrors); publish sensitivity, specificity, and agreement separately for each parasomnia subtype to flag morphology-specific algorithm weaknesses.
review: Controls trade-off between annotation efficiency (sensitivity) and specificity to true parasomnia events. Weakness: The options assume sufficient labeled training data exists by month 12–18 to reliably train stage-two filters, but parasomnia heterogeneity within the initial cohort may be too sparse to generalize, risking unstable algorithm performance if early enrollment skews toward one parasomnia subtype.

name: Data Governance and Publication Sequencing
consequences: The protocol permits de-identified physiological data deposition in public repositories but restricts bedroom video due to privacy constraints; this asymmetry creates a decision about what granularity of event context (raw signals, annotated event windows, or aggregated phenotypic summaries) is released versus retained for competitive advantage or regulatory compliance. Early aggressive data sharing accelerates reproducibility and collaboration but risks premature critique of preliminary methods; delayed release maximizes publication momentum but raises ethical questions about withholding data from the field.
options: Release de-identified raw polysomnography montages and extracted event windows (sensor data only, no video) on a rolling basis as they are dual-rated and incorporated into the main analysis, with a 6-month embargo from publication date; this maximizes transparency and enables rapid independent reanalysis while protecting methodological novelty during the critical publication phase.

Adopt a staged release model: methods papers and phenotypic summaries are published and data released at month 24; algorithmic triage details and raw event-window data are released at month 36 after the Aim 3 benchmark is finalized; retain baseline actigraphy and sensor activity summaries for comparison cohort indefinitely for participant privacy reasons.

Establish a tiered access protocol with an institutional data access committee: open release of fully anonymized summary statistics and aggregated event phenotypes immediately after publication; restricted conditional access (data use agreement, institutional affiliation required) for raw polysomnography and sensor streams; no video release under any circumstance, with participants explicitly informed of this boundary at enrollment.
review: Controls timing and scope of data transparency vs. competitive publication advantage and privacy risk mitigation. Weakness: The options do not specify how to handle incidental findings (e.g., unsuspected sleep apnea or epileptiform activity detected during scoring) that may warrant clinical disclosure to participants and their physicians, which could conflict with de-identification protocols and data governance choices.

name: Institutional Partnership Depth and Pharma Collaboration Scope
consequences: The protocol identifies pharma partnership as 'optional upside' with the partner receiving only a 30-day pre-publication comment window; this deliberately weak coupling minimizes conflicts of interest but also forgoes potential supplementary funding and commercial validation. Deeper partnership (co-authorship, joint algorithm development, or device manufacturing interest) could accelerate iteration but risks agenda misalignment, publication delays, or pressure to prematurely productize methods not yet ready for clinical deployment.
options: Maintain strict independence: recruit funding exclusively from DFG and University of Bonn; if pharma interest emerges, formalize it only as a named sponsor with zero input rights, transparent in acknowledgments, and ensure the PI retains unilateral publication authority and timeline control.

Establish a co-development partnership with a single pharma or medtech firm interested in future drug-device-combination trials; negotiate joint Aim 3 work on the event-triage algorithm, with the partner contributing algorithm engineers and computing resources in exchange for a joint methods patent and non-exclusive license for future clinical-trial applications; retain academic publication rights and 6-month pre-launch exclusivity for academic papers.

Create a 'methods feedback board' including one interested pharma representative, one independent sleep physician, and one patient advocate; this board reviews progress every 6 months and provides non-binding technical input on algorithm design and phenotyping priorities, formalized as advisory input only with no decision-making power, allowing external perspective without institutional entanglement.
review: Controls autonomy of research direction and publication timing vs. access to industry expertise and potential future clinical translation pathways. Weakness: The options do not address how to manage conflicts of interest if a pharma partner has competing interests in parasomnia treatment products, nor do they specify exit mechanisms if partnership priorities diverge from the academic science agenda.

name: External Scrutiny Cadence and Adaptive Protocol Authority
consequences: The governance plan includes a 3-member scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months and a protocol-activation rule: if by month 18 enrollment is below 25 participants or captured events are below 40, external review is triggered. This structure provides accountability but creates decision-making friction if the board identifies critical design flaws (e.g., recruitment-criteria exclusions are too stringent, or sensor false-alarm rates are unsustainable) mid-study; rapid protocol iteration may be needed but requires balancing board authority against PI operational continuity.
options: Establish a 'rapid-response modification framework': the PI may implement protocol amendments addressing recruitment, technician staffing, sensor calibration, or scoring criteria without board pre-approval, provided that: (a) amendments are documented prospectively, (b) cumulative changes do not exceed three per 6-month period, and (c) all changes are reviewed by the board at the next scheduled meeting; this preserves agility while maintaining transparency.

Institute board-delegated authority: designate one board member as a standing 'operations liaison' with monthly check-in calls; this liaison has authority to approve technical operational changes (e.g., sensor upgrades, shift scheduling, minor inclusion-criteria refinements) on behalf of the board, with written confirmation, reducing latency for adaptive response while maintaining institutional oversight.

Adopt a pre-specified contingency-response protocol: before enrollment begins, develop written decision rules for specific triggering events (e.g., if event-capture rate falls below X% by month 12, move to extended-stay protocol; if recruitment stalls below Y per month for two consecutive months, activate secondary recruitment channels); contingencies are automatically executed by the PI without board pre-approval but flagged in monthly dashboards, reducing uncertainty and decision latency.
review: Controls balance between operational agility and external accountability, determining who modifies protocol in real time. Weakness: The options assume that pre-specified decision rules or liaison authority can be detailed sufficiently before enrollment to cover genuine emergencies, but unforeseen methodological failures (e.g., systematic artifact in a sensor modality, or unexpected safety incident) may require judgment calls not covered by predetermined rules, leaving ambiguity about escalation.

name: Matched Control Recruitment and Baseline Normalization Strategy
consequences: The protocol requires a matched control group to benchmark event-triage false-positive rates and establish baseline nocturnal movement and arousal patterns under identical residential monitoring; however, recruiting healthy controls for a 2–8 week unpaid stay (or minimal compensation) is logistically challenging and recruitment timeline may lag case enrollment, delaying algorithm validation and Aim 2 comparisons. Control data quality and representativeness also depend critically on matching criteria (age, sex, sleep duration, comorbidity burden), and inadequate matching may confound signal interpretation.
options: Recruit controls proactively in year one via convenience sampling (university staff, hospital employees, student population) and complete their stays before case recruitment accelerates; aim for n=20 controls by month 9 to enable early baseline calibration of sensor false-alarm rates and provide a stable reference for algorithm training, accepting age and demographic skew inherent in convenience sampling and reporting sample characteristics transparently.

Implement 'matched-pair recruitment': for every parasomnia case enrolled, prospectively identify and recruit a control matched on age (±5 years), sex, and habitual sleep duration from the same geographic referral region; stagger control admissions to occur within 1–3 months of matched case stay, ensuring contemporary baseline data and environmental consistency, accepting slower enrollment velocity but gaining demographic fidelity.

Use historical control data from prior residential polysomnography studies conducted at University Hospital Bonn (if available) or partner sleep labs to establish preliminary baseline norms for movement, arousal, and sensor artifact under similar residential monitoring; enroll only 5–10 prospective controls during the study for algorithm validation, reducing recruitment burden and allowing flexibility in control characteristics while validating against existing normative datasets.
review: Controls efficiency of control recruitment and fidelity of baseline normalization for algorithm development vs. operational complexity and recruitment timeline risk. Weakness: The options do not address how to stratify control matching by potential sleep disorders (e.g., ensuring controls have normal sleep architecture and no history of sleep-related movement) or how missing control data impacts the interpretability of Aim 3 algorithm benchmarks if case-control comparisons are incomplete by the 36-month deadline.

name: Residential Authenticity Preservation versus Safety-Monitoring Trade-off
consequences: Deciding how much clinical apparatus to embed in suites (e.g., bed rails, ceiling-mounted cameras, wired electrode leads) directly affects both the naturalness of nocturnal behavior and the speed of technician response to events. Excessive clinical design suppresses parasomnia expression and requires longer stays to capture events; minimal design increases event detection but may slow staff response in a crisis, complicating incident reporting and insurance compliance.
options: Embed all monitoring infrastructure invisibly via mattress sensors, ambient microphones, and motion-capture mounted in ceiling voids and walls, reserving visible PSG leads only for the three scheduled enhanced nights, forcing technicians to rely on sensor alerts for rapid location and triage.

Install modular clinical stations (bed rails, grab bars, pulse oximetry ports) that are visually integrated into bespoke furniture so participants perceive them as residential comfort aids rather than medical apparatus, allowing staff to deploy clinical monitoring on demand without prior aesthetic compromise.

Maintain two distinct suite designs—four suites furnished as clinical bedrooms with full visible apparatus and rapid-deploy PSG for high-risk or frequent-event participants, and four as minimalist residential rooms for lower-risk participants, requiring protocol logic to assign participants to suite type at admission.
review: Controls residential aesthetics vs. clinical responsiveness. Weakness: The options underestimate the staff training burden—technicians in minimal-infrastructure suites need faster pattern recognition skills and may require more sensor-literacy training, which trades off against hire-speed and onboarding timelines.

name: Capture-Rate Yield Strategy and Participant Duration Elasticity
consequences: The protocol permits initial 2–8 week stays with up to 2-week extension if zero events are captured, capped at 10 weeks and a 20% unproductive-admission ceiling. Extending stays boosts event yield (more nights = higher probability of capturing infrequent events) but increases per-participant cost and housing throughput pressure; tightening the window reduces cost per participant but increases the unproductive-admission rate and threatens minimum event counts for Aim 2 analysis.
options: Set a fixed 4-week baseline stay for all NREM participants with extension offered only to those with clinically severe self-reported episode frequency (≥4 per month), reserving the extended-stay slots for the control group and RBD protocol to manage housing inventory.

Implement adaptive stay duration based on real-time capture: if a participant captures an event by week 3, discharge by week 4; if zero captures by week 3, automatically extend to week 6 and reassess, using sensor-derived arousal clustering to predict whether extension is likely to yield events.

Standardize all NREM participants to a 6-week stay baseline with no individual extensions, absorbing the increased cost per participant as a reduction in the year-one target cohort size (12–15 participants instead of 15–20) to reach the year-two ramp within budget and maintain controllable false-alarm and annotation load.
review: Controls cost-efficiency vs. methodological completeness. Weakness: None of the options account for seasonal variation in parasomnia frequency or participant motivation decay in weeks 6–8, both of which may inflate the unproductive-admission rate independent of capture strategy.

name: Event-Annotation Throughput and Dual-Scorer Consensus Model
consequences: The protocol mandates two independent raters applying AASM criteria to all flagged events and reports inter-rater reliability; this ensures rigorous benchmarking of the semi-automated triage tool but creates a bottleneck—each event requires four human-review hours (technician annotation plus two scorer reviews). Accepting lower inter-rater agreement thresholds or narrowing the events sent for dual scoring accelerates analysis but weakens the evidentiary foundation for Aim 3 algorithm validation and publication credibility.
options: Require dual scoring only for events flagged by the semi-automated triage tool at medium-to-high confidence (e.g., top 80% of ranked candidates), sending all other flagged events to single-scorer review, reducing dual-review volume by ~30% and calculating inter-rater reliability on the high-confidence subset only.

Hire a dedicated third scorer—a senior sleep technologist or neurophysiologist—who adjudicates disagreements between the two primary scorers immediately after initial review, establishing a consensus ground-truth in real-time and avoiding analysis delays while maintaining full dual-scoring rigor.

Develop a rapid three-question triage pre-screen (event onset, limb movement clarity, arousal visibility) applied by the night technician before events reach formal scoring, rejecting obvious false-positives (e.g., voluntary repositioning) at the filter stage so only high-likelihood candidates enter dual-scoring, reducing submitted volume by ~40%.
review: Controls annotation rigor vs. workflow velocity. Weakness: The options ignore quality drift—without centralized scoring oversight or periodic calibration sessions, inter-rater reliability may degrade by month 9–12 as scorers apply criteria differently in isolation, requiring more rigorous QA checkpoints than the options specify.

name: Sensor Redundancy and Data-Loss Mitigation Versus Infrastructure Complexity
consequences: The tiered acquisition model (low-burden nightly monitoring plus enhanced PSG on three nights per stay) generates multiple data streams (EEG headbands, mattress sensors, cameras, microphones, environmental sensors, scheduled full PSG) that must be time-synchronized, stored, and backed up. Increasing sensor redundancy and stricter synchronization protocols capture richer phenotyping data and reduce loss risk, but amplify data-management overhead, require more network infrastructure, and increase the likelihood of synchronization artifacts that confound analysis.
options: Deploy a single authoritative time source (network-synchronized atomic clock or GPS receiver) feeding all sensors via a hardware trigger signal, with a dedicated validation pipeline that flags time-skew >100 milliseconds before data enters analysis, accepting a 5–10% data-rejection rate for maximum confidence in true temporal alignment.

Implement software-only synchronization using post-hoc timestamp alignment against annotated physiological events (e.g., matched EOG blinks across cameras and EEG), reducing infrastructure cost and complexity but requiring longer post-collection processing and making synchronization unverifiable if events are genuinely absent.

Install redundant independent time sources on a subset of data streams (e.g., EEG headband and camera each with local oscillators, plus network sync as tertiary), logging drift automatically and reconciling timestamps after collection, distributing the synchronization burden across multiple sensors and reducing single-point failure risk.
review: Controls data integrity vs. operational simplicity. Weakness: All options assume stable local-area network conditions; if facility WiFi or wired connections degrade (likely during renovations or in the first 6 months), synchronization will fail silently or produce artifacts misattributed to physiology, requiring more explicit network monitoring than any option prescribes.

name: Matched-Control Recruitment Pathway and Baseline Normalization
consequences: A matched control group captures normal nocturnal behavior (movement, arousals, sensor false-positives) to benchmark the event-triage tool and discriminate pathological from physiological signals. Strict matching (age, sex, sleep duration, occupational stress) and longer control stays (to accumulate comparable sensor hours) yield higher-quality baselines but consume recruitment effort, housing capacity, and budget that could instead enroll more patient participants with actual parasomnia events, directly reducing Aim 2 phenotyping sample size.
options: Recruit controls from the University Hospital Bonn general population (hospital staff, student volunteers, family members of other trial participants) with minimal matching criteria beyond age range 18–65, enrolling 8–10 controls with 1–2 week stays to generate sufficient sensor hours at minimal recruitment burden, then weight statistical models for covariate imbalance at analysis stage.

Perform strict 1:1 matching on age decade, sex, and self-reported sleep quality, recruiting controls from the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin membership network with 3–4 week stays (matched to the patient baseline), requiring explicit pre-enrollment screening to verify absence of undiagnosed parasomnia symptoms via questionnaire.

Reserve the matched-control enrollment for year two after NREM parasomnia recruitment has stabilized, using year-one participant data to retrospectively identify their closest hospital-staff or volunteer matches enrolled during the same month, treating the year-one control data as exploratory and the year-two cohort as the formal benchmarking baseline.
review: Controls methodological rigor vs. recruitment efficiency. Weakness: All options defer the question of whether family-member controls (e.g., bed partners of NREM participants) introduce hidden bias—cohabitation with a parasomnia patient may suppress control sleep or increase control arousal, contaminating the baseline, yet none of the options explicitly exclude or monitor this confound.

name: Pharma Partnership Scope and Publication Autonomy Boundary
consequences: Securing pharma co-funding would provide flexibility in year-two expansion and algorithm-development timelines but introduces negotiation over publication timing, data access, and acknowledgment of commercial interest. The current protocol grants pharma a 30-day pre-publication review window only; tightening this (e.g., no pharma veto, immediate publication) preserves scientific independence but may deter partnership; expanding it (e.g., 90-day window, joint manuscript approvals) risks funding-driven delays and perception of bias in results interpretation.
options: Explicitly prohibit pharma partnership at the outset, relying entirely on DFG and University of Bonn funding, ensuring unconditional publication rights and regulatory independence, and accepting the consequence that year-two expansion and algorithm-development timelines may slip by 6–12 months if contingency budget is consumed by staffing or facility repairs.

Negotiate a narrowly scoped pharma partnership limited to algorithm-development costs (e.g., €200K for computational support) rather than core operations, with a written clause stating that pharma receives no data access, no authorship eligibility, and a publication policy identical to academic co-authors (standard 4-week pre-print review only).

Open partnership discussions with 2–3 sleep-medicine or neurology-focused pharmaceutical firms, contingent on them accepting a joint governance structure where the academic PI retains unilateral publication authority and pharmacokinetic/biomarker data related to any investigational drugs are excluded from pharma analysis, requiring explicit written separation-of-concerns in the contract.
review: Controls scientific autonomy vs. funding stability. Weakness: The options overlook reputational risk—even with explicit contractual firewalls, accepting pharma funding in sleep disorders (a field where medications are marketed to patients) creates implicit perception of bias in phenotyping or algorithm design, which may require proactive disclosure in publications and advisory-board review that the options do not mandate.

name: External Advisory-Board Authority and Adaptive Protocol Revision Cadence
consequences: The governance structure includes a 3-member external advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. More frequent board engagement (quarterly) and broader revision authority (e.g., lowering the €50K threshold or permitting the board to halt enrollment pending safety review) slows decision-making but strengthens oversight of adverse events and methodological drift; less frequent meetings and narrower authority accelerate on-the-fly problem-solving but increase the risk of undetected protocol violations or scope creep.
options: Establish a lightweight monthly online advisory-board check-in (15–30 minutes) to review enrollment, adverse-event summaries, and data-quality metrics, with formal in-person meetings at 6-month intervals for substantive re-evaluation, keeping the €50K budget-authority threshold but granting the board expedited halt-authority if cumulative adverse events exceed 3 in a rolling 90-day window.

Restrict advisory-board engagement to quarterly formal review meetings (instead of 6-monthly), each requiring the PI to submit a written progress report and data dashboard 1 week in advance, allowing the board to request protocol amendments with 14-day turnaround for PI response, reducing friction between meetings but requiring more administrative overhead.

Empower the external board to unilaterally halt participant enrollment for 14 days if safety concerns are raised, without requiring PI sign-off, then escalate to the University of Bonn research ethics committee if the PI and board cannot agree within the pause window, introducing a faster escalation pathway for unresolved conflicts but increasing the risk of unnecessary enrollment freezes based on isolated events.
review: Controls PI autonomy vs. external oversight depth. Weakness: The options assume external board members will be reliably available for monthly or quarterly meetings, but they typically volunteer on time-constrained academic schedules; if any board member misses two consecutive meetings due to sabbatical or career transitions, the governance framework degrades unless replacement procedures are pre-specified, which none of the options address.
